owasp_top10: OWASP IoT T10:2018 - I3: Insecure Ecosystem Interfaces
OWASP IoT T10:2018 - I1: Weak, Guessable, or Hardcoded Passwords
OWASP IoT T10:2018 - I5: Use of Insecure or Outdated Components
cwe: CWE-345 - Insufficient Verification of Data Authenticity

threat.title: IoT Firmware Privilege Escalation via Binary Exploitation
threat.description: IoT devices running embedded Linux are vulnerable to privilege escalation attacks through buffer overflow exploits, insecure firmware update mechanisms lacking signature verification, UART/JTAG debug interface exposure, and setuid binary exploitation. Attackers can gain root shell access to compromise the entire device, pivot to network infrastructure, and create persistent backdoors. This threat is particularly critical as IoT devices often run outdated firmware with known vulnerabilities and lack security updates due to end-of-life support.

attack.scenario:
1. Attacker performs reconnaissance using binwalk, firmware-mod-kit, and ghidra to extract and analyze firmware from public sources or device dump
2. Identifies buffer overflow vulnerability in web interface CGI binary (e.g., dnscfg.cgi) or command injection in UPnP service
3. Gains physical access to UART interface (TX/RX/GND pins) using USB-to-TTL adapter and minicom at 115200 baud
4. Exploits insecure firmware update mechanism by crafting malicious firmware without valid signature verification
5. Escalates privileges via setuid binary exploitation (busybox, su, sudo) or kernel vulnerability (CVE-2024-1086)
6. Achieves persistent root shell access, disables security features, installs backdoor, and pivots to internal network
prerequisites: IoT device running embedded Linux with known vulnerabilities
Insecure firmware update mechanism without signature verification
Exposed UART/JTAG debug interfaces with no authentication
Vulnerable setuid binaries or outdated kernel with privilege escalation CVEs
No secure boot or firmware integrity checking enabled

cve_references.description: D-Link DSL routers command injection in dnscfg.cgi allowing unauthenticated RCE as root
Linux kernel nf_tables use-after-free vulnerability enabling privilege escalation (Flipping Pages)
Integer overflow in create_elf_tables() allowing unprivileged user to escalate via SUID binary
Blink router command injection allowing remote code execution and device takeover
AVTECH IP camera elevated privilege command injection compromising device security
Edimax IP camera buffer overflow exploited by Mirai botnet for privilege escalation

real_world_breaches.impact: Thousands of end-of-life D-Link DSL routers compromised via command injection, enabling botnet recruitment and network infiltration
real_world_breaches.root_cause: Improper sanitization of DNS configuration parameters in dnscfg.cgi CGI endpoint combined with lack of security updates for EOL devices

real_world_breaches.incident: AVTECH IP Camera Mirai Botnet Campaign
real_world_breaches.impact: Unpatched AVTECH IP cameras exploited since 2019 to spread Mirai malware, creating global DDoS botnet compromising over 100,000 devices
real_world_breaches.root_cause: Known vulnerability from 2019 only assigned CVE in 2024, no patch available for discontinued products

real_world_breaches.impact: Thousands of smart locks, cameras, and thermostats compromised via weak passwords and firmware vulnerabilities, resulting in privacy breaches and unauthorized physical access
real_world_breaches.root_cause: Default credentials, lack of firmware updates, and privilege escalation exploits in embedded web servers

real_world_breaches.impact: Amazon Alexa and Google Assistant devices compromised via spoofed commands exploiting firmware vulnerabilities, granting attackers control over smart homes
real_world_breaches.root_cause: Insufficient authentication in voice command processing and privilege escalation via embedded Linux kernel exploits

real_world_breaches.impact: Global botnet of 500,000+ IoT devices created using Mirai variants, exploiting known firmware vulnerabilities for DDoS attacks
real_world_breaches.root_cause: Buffer overflow and privilege escalation in outdated firmware, lack of secure boot and signature verification

bug_bounty_reports.summary: Privilege escalation via UART root shell access in consumer router firmware due to missing authentication
Buffer overflow in firmware update mechanism allowing unsigned firmware installation and root access

security_research.title: Debugging D-Link: Emulating Firmware and Hacking Hardware
security_research.key_finding: UART exploitation and firmware analysis revealed systemic privilege escalation vulnerabilities in D-Link router firmware

security_research.title: Netgear Router and IoT Firmware Security & Vulnerabilities
security_research.key_finding: 81 kernel space exploits identified in Netgear firmware Linux kernel, enabling privilege escalation to root

security_research.title: IoT Threat Report 2023
security_research.key_finding: Buffer overflow (28.25%) and privilege escalation vulnerabilities are the most common in IoT devices, with TVs representing 34% of vulnerable devices

security_research.title: Active Exploitation of Linux Kernel Privilege Escalation (CVE-2024-1086)
security_research.key_finding: Ransomware groups exploiting CVE-2024-1086 kernel vulnerability for privilege escalation in IoT and embedded systems

mitigations.title: Implement Secure Firmware Updates with Signature Verification (ESP-IDF)
mitigations.description: Deploy cryptographic signature verification for all firmware updates using ESP-IDF Secure Boot v2 with RSA-PSS verification. This ensures only authenticated firmware signed with private keys can be installed on devices, preventing malicious firmware injection and downgrade attacks.
nist_csf: PR.IP-1 - Baseline configuration of IT/OT is created and maintained
PR.DS-6 - Integrity checking mechanisms are used
validation_steps: Enable CONFIG_SECURE_BOOT_V2_ENABLED in ESP-IDF menuconfig
Generate RSA-3072 signing key using espsecure.py
Build firmware with secure boot enabled
Test signature verification with valid and invalid signatures
Verify A/B partition fallback on signature failure
Confirm anti-rollback protection blocks older firmware versions

mitigations.title: Disable UART/JTAG Debug Interfaces in Production
mitigations.description: Disable or authenticate access to UART, JTAG, and other hardware debug interfaces to prevent physical privilege escalation attacks. Use eFuse bits to permanently disable debug access in production devices.
iso27001_controls: A.11.2.6 - Security of equipment and assets off-premises
A.9.1.2 - Access to networks and network services
nist_csf: PR.PT-3 - Access to systems is controlled
validation_steps: Burn eFuse bits to disable JTAG/UART in production firmware
Test physical access via UART (should be blocked)
Verify bootloader messages are disabled
Confirm ROM download mode is disabled
Test firmware cannot be dumped via UART/JTAG

mitigations.title: Implement Privilege Separation and Least Privilege
mitigations.description: Run IoT services with minimal privileges, avoid setuid binaries, and implement mandatory access control (MAC) using SELinux or AppArmor. Drop root privileges immediately after initialization.
iso27001_controls: A.9.2.3 - Management of privileged access rights
validation_steps: Create unprivileged iotservice user and group
Test service starts as root and drops to iotservice user
Verify service cannot escalate back to root
Confirm only required capabilities are set (CAP_NET_BIND_SERVICE)
Test that no_new_privs prevents exploitation of setuid binaries

mitigations.title: Enable Stack Protection and ASLR
mitigations.description: Compile firmware with stack canaries, ASLR, DEP/NX, and RELRO to mitigate buffer overflow exploitation. Use modern compiler flags for exploit mitigation.

code_example.code:
# Makefile for Secure IoT Firmware Build
# Implements exploit mitigation compiler flags

CC = gcc
CFLAGS = -Wall -Wextra -Werror

# Stack protection (stack canaries)
CFLAGS += -fstack-protector-strong
# Even stronger: -fstack-protector-all (for all functions)

# Position Independent Executable (PIE) for ASLR
CFLAGS += -fPIE
LDFLAGS += -pie

# Enable DEP/NX (non-executable stack)
LDFLAGS += -Wl,-z,noexecstack

# Enable full RELRO (Read-Only Relocations)
LDFLAGS += -Wl,-z,relro,-z,now

# Format string protection
CFLAGS += -Wformat -Wformat-security
CFLAGS += -D_FORTIFY_SOURCE=2

# Buffer overflow detection
CFLAGS += -D_GLIBCXX_ASSERTIONS

# Control Flow Integrity (if supported)
# CFLAGS += -fcf-protection=full  # Intel CET
# CFLAGS += -fsanitize=cfi        # Clang CFI

# Shadow stack (if supported by architecture)
# CFLAGS += -mshstk

# Additional hardening for production
ifeq ($(BUILD_TYPE),production)
    # Strip debug symbols
    LDFLAGS += -Wl,-s
    
    # Optimize for size and security
    CFLAGS += -Os -ffunction-sections -fdata-sections
    LDFLAGS += -Wl,--gc-sections
    
    # Disable debugging features
    CFLAGS += -DNDEBUG
endif

# Example targets
TARGET = iot_firmware
SOURCES = main.c network.c crypto.c
OBJECTS = $(SOURCES:.c=.o)

all: $(TARGET)

$(TARGET): $(OBJECTS)
	$(CC) $(OBJECTS) $(LDFLAGS) -o $(TARGET)
	@echo "Firmware built with security hardening"
	@checksec --file=$(TARGET)

%.o: %.c
	$(CC) $(CFLAGS) -c $< -o $@

clean:
	rm -f $(OBJECTS) $(TARGET)

# Verify security features are enabled
check-security:
	@echo "Checking security features..."
	@readelf -l $(TARGET) | grep -q "GNU_STACK.*RW" && echo "✓ NX/DEP enabled" || echo "✗ NX/DEP missing"
	@readelf -d $(TARGET) | grep -q "BIND_NOW" && echo "✓ Full RELRO enabled" || echo "✗ Full RELRO missing"
	@readelf -h $(TARGET) | grep -q "DYN" && echo "✓ PIE enabled" || echo "✗ PIE missing"
	@objdump -d $(TARGET) | grep -q "__stack_chk_fail" && echo "✓ Stack canaries enabled" || echo "✗ Stack canaries missing"

.PHONY: all clean check-security

nist_csf: PR.IP-1 - Baseline configuration is created and maintained
validation_steps: Update Makefile with security compiler flags
Rebuild firmware with hardening enabled
Run checksec to verify NX, PIE, RELRO, stack canaries
Test binary cannot be exploited with basic buffer overflow
Confirm ASLR is enabled in kernel (cat /proc/sys/kernel/randomize_va_space)

indicators: Unusual firmware update attempts from unauthorized sources
Unexpected privilege escalation events in system logs
New setuid binaries or modified permissions on critical files
Kernel module loading outside normal boot process
Abnormal CPU/memory usage patterns indicating exploit attempts
Network connections to command-and-control servers from IoT devices
Modified bootloader or firmware integrity check failures

log_queries.query:
// Detect IoT device privilege escalation attempts
let PrivilegedCommands = dynamic(['sudo', 'su', 'setuid', 'setgid', 'chmod +s']);
Syslog
| where Computer contains "iot-" or Computer contains "router-" or Computer contains "camera-"
| where Facility == "authpriv" or Facility == "security"
| where SyslogMessage has_any (PrivilegedCommands)
| extend CommandExecuted = extract(@"command\s*=\s*([^\s]+)", 1, SyslogMessage)
| extend User = extract(@"user\s*=\s*([^\s]+)", 1, SyslogMessage)
| where User != "root"
| summarize AttemptCount = count(), FirstSeen = min(TimeGenerated), LastSeen = max(TimeGenerated) by Computer, User, CommandExecuted
| where AttemptCount > 3
| order by AttemptCount desc

event.category: "process" AND 
(process.name: ("sudo" OR "su" OR "busybox") OR process.args: ("chmod +s" OR "setuid" OR "setcap")) AND 
host.type: "iot" AND 
user.name: * AND NOT user.name: "root" AND 
event.outcome: "success"

index=iot_logs sourcetype=syslog 
(process_name="sudo" OR process_name="su" OR process_name="busybox" OR command="*chmod +s*" OR command="*setuid*")
user!=root 
event_outcome=success
| stats count as escalation_attempts by host, user, process_name, command, _time
| where escalation_attempts > 3
| sort - escalation_attempts

fields @timestamp, deviceId, event.type, event.user, event.command
| filter event.type = "privilege_escalation" or event.command like /sudo|su|setuid/
| filter event.user != "root"
| stats count() as attempts by deviceId, event.user, event.command
| filter attempts > 3
| sort attempts desc
